result:
There is a peculiar kind of comfort in looking up at the night sky and realizing how infinitesimally small we truly are. We spend our days tangled in the minutiae of existence—worrying about unanswered emails, paying utility bills, navigating rush hour traffic, or stressing over exactly what to say in a meeting. We carry these burdens as though they are the absolute center of the universe, because to us, they are. But step outside on a clear night and look up. That pale band of light stretching across the darkness is the Milky Way, a cosmic metropolis of hundreds of billions of stars, many harboring their own planets, their own histories, their own untold stories. And our sun is just one of those stars, situated on a quiet spiral arm, far from the galactic downtown.

The light hitting your eyes right now is ancient. It has traveled for thousands, sometimes millions, of years just to terminate on the retinas of a fleeting creature on a spinning rock. By the time you see a star, it might already be dead, leaving behind only a ghost of photons. It is a deeply humbling realization, but not a depressing one. It means our mistakes are temporary, our anxieties are manageable, and our shared existence is a rare, beautiful anomaly. In the vast, cold expanse of the cosmos, the fact that we exist at all—that we can look up and wonder—is a miracle. We are the universe thinking about itself.
